source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

3. The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

4. Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

image_dependency: Question refers to figure-specific geometry and labeled points/components (M, N, O, quarter-circular analyzer, wafer coordinates, field directions relative to the paper).
multi_step: The question has four explicit sub-questions.
Solving requires chaining multiple physics relations across stages: velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, then deflection system.
The final parts require comparing/combining electric-only, magnetic-only, and simultaneous-field cases.
The answer consists of multiple derived symbolic expressions rather than a single lookup result.
verifiability: answer_present=true with explicit symbolic results for v, q/m, and three coordinate pairs.
The requested outputs are well-structured mathematical quantities, which can be checked by derivation or symbolic equivalence.
Some verification complexity remains because answer_type=set and symbolic expressions may need normalization.

decision_reasons: Core assets are present and usable: question text, answer, and primary image all exist, and registry_passed=true.
Initial scoring is strong across image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability, indicating high candidate value.
The problem appears to rely on figure-specific physics/geometry and includes structured symbolic answers, making it a strong fit for the candidate pool.
The recorded issue is a metadata/path inconsistency rather than a loss of core semantic content.
Verification may require symbolic equivalence handling, but this is a downstream checking complexity, not a reason to exclude the sample.

Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. clean_problem_record.normalized_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

targets.text: Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio.

When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper).
Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.

Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

node_records.canonical_value:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.

In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem statement is fully specified and the expected answers are clear. The diagram is needed for the geometry, field directions, and wafer coordinate orientation, and it is readable enough to support the text. Despite some dense spatial setup, the text-image pair is semantically understandable.

cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is semantically usable: question and answer are complete, the rewrite is usable, and solvability is strong. However, alignment is marked risky with a recorded conflict, and there is a non-fatal image-path inconsistency alongside spatially complex visual setup, so manual review is warranted rather than an automatic pass.

conflict_pairs.detail: 文本中的视觉锚点明显多于可识别视觉区域。

field_audit_records.before_value:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

3. The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

4. Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.
